flashcards.front: Define Stack ADT and its fundamental principle
flashcards.back: Stack is a linear abstract data type following LIFO (Last In, First Out) principle. The two primary operations are push (insert at top) and pop (remove from top), allowing access only to the most recently inserted element.

Using a fixed-size array with a top pointer initialized to -1. Push increments top and stores element; Pop returns element at top then decrements top. Overflow occurs when top equals MAXSIZE-1; underflow when top equals -1.

flashcards.front: Linked List-based Stack Implementation in C
flashcards.back: Each node contains data and pointer to next node. Push creates new node, links to current top, updates top. Pop frees top node and updates top to next. No overflow (dynamic size) but requires extra memory for pointers.

flashcards.front: Time Complexity Analysis for Stack Operations
flashcards.back: For both array and linked list implementations: Push, Pop, and Peek (top) operations take O(1) constant time. This is because we only modify/access the top element without traversing the entire structure.

flashcards.front: Space Complexity: Array vs Linked List Stack
flashcards.back: Array implementation: O(n) fixed space where n is MAXSIZE. Linked list implementation: O(n) dynamic space but with overhead—each node stores data plus pointer (typically 4-8 bytes). Linked list uses more memory per element.

flashcards.front: Practical Applications of Stacks in Computing
flashcards.back: Stacks enable: (1) Function call management in compilers/runtime, (2) Expression evaluation and syntax parsing, (3) Undo mechanisms in editors, (4) Backtracking algorithms, (5) Browser history navigation.

Overflow: Attempting push when stack is full (array implementation reaches capacity). Underflow: Attempting pop or peek when stack is empty. Proper validation with isFull() and isEmpty() functions is essential before operations.

flashcards.front: When to choose Array vs Linked List Stack Implementation
flashcards.back: Choose Array when: size is known/fixed, memory is constrained, O(1) random access needed. Choose Linked List when: size is unknown/variable, frequent insertions/deletions, no memory wastage. Array offers cache locality; linked list offers flexibility.
